claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit. I'll need to ask you a few questions to guide you through the process.

**First, let's start with your child's age:**

Are you claiming for a child who is:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **Between 16-19 years old**, or
- **20 years or older**?

Please tell me your child's age (or which age category they fall into).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 8 years old and lives with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I assume he's not working at all, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all - he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming that. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in his own name. 

Given that Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I assume he's not receiving any benefits in his own right - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT in any apprenticeship program?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I assume he's not in any apprenticeship program. Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 8, so he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. This is an important eligibility requirement.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, or
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold), or
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days), or
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself, or
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources, or
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national, or
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most commonly, people qualify simply by normally living in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK (in Manchester), you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex**, or
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.), or
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives with you at home in Manchester, so it sounds like you meet this requirement. Can you confirm that you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives here at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex lives with you, you are responsible for him. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member or guardian
- Anyone else who might be responsible for Alex

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living situation?**

- **Living normally at home** (with you in Manchester)
- In hospital
- In local authority care
- Being fostered

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - a few months. But he's still my child and I'm hoping to claim for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand, and I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. You can still claim Child Benefit for a child in hospital, but there are time limits that apply. Let me check the details.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I need to understand the timing of Alex's hospital stay.

**Has Alex been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (cumulatively)?**

The rules work like this:
- Child Benefit can continue for up to 12 weeks while a child is in hospital
- If Alex has had multiple hospital stays, they count together (cumulatively) if the gap between stays is less than 28 days
- If the gap is 28 days or more, the counter resets

You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for "a few months" - can you tell me:
1. How long has Alex been in hospital continuously? 
2. Has he had any breaks from hospital during this time?

This will help me determine if he's been there for more or less than 12 weeks total.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital continuously for about 11 weeks now, so just under the 12 week limit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks (which is under the 12-week limit), let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - since Alex has been in hospital for under 12 weeks, you can continue to claim Child Benefit during this time. However, I need to ask one more question:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he is in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or personal items for him
- Travel costs to visit him
- Treats or gifts
- Any other regular expenses for his benefit

Are you regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy things for him - clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks. I want to make sure he has what he needs while he's there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's wonderful that you're continuing to support Alex during his hospital stay. Since you are regularly spending money on him, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now, one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's care or accommodation costs while he's in hospital? 

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything - it's all through the NHS hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's great news. Since the council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, and his hospital care is through the NHS, you meet all the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✓

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex.

### Why you qualify:
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he automatically qualifies as a child
- You live in the UK (Manchester), meeting the residency requirements
- You live with Alex and are responsible for him
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Even though Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on him (clothes, magazines, snacks), which means you can continue to claim
- The local council is not paying towards Alex's care (it's through the NHS)

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments:** Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important about Alex's hospital stay:
Please note that since Alex is approaching the 12-week limit, if he remains in hospital beyond 12 weeks total, you'll need to continue regularly spending money on him to maintain your eligibility for Child Benefit.

**You can find more information and apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers soon! Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 years old - the automatic qualifying age; (2) He is not in paid work; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks (under the 12-week limit), the claimant continues to regularly spend money on him (clothes, magazines, snacks), which allows the claim to continue; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his care is through the NHS. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 11 weeks, the claimant meets all requirements including UK residency, living with and being responsible for Alex, regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay, and no local council payments being made. No other person is claiming for Alex.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 years old - the automatic qualifying age; (2) He is not in paid work; (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (6) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks (under the 12-week limit), the claimant continues to regularly spend money on him (clothes, magazines, snacks), which allows the claim to continue; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his care is through the NHS. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 11 weeks, the claimant meets all requirements including UK residency, living with and being responsible for Alex, regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay, and no local council payments being made. No other person is claiming for Alex.
